clean_story:
Cause and reason. A word that explains why.

The term because comes from Old English bī cause, meaning "by the cause of"—a bridge between actions and their reasons. It’s like a lever: pull it, and the hidden gears of logic click into place.

Because doesn’t just answer how; it uncovers why. A child asks, "Why is the sky blue?" The answer starts with because—light scatters, molecules bend, physics unfolds. Without it, explanations stall. Courts demand it ("motive"), scientists chase it ("causation"), poets twist it ("because I could not stop for Death").

Yet because can also mislead. Say "I tripped because the floor was wet." True—but what if you were distracted? Causes stack like invisible layers. Philosophers call this the "regress problem": every because begets another, forever.

Three related subjects are causal chains, logical fallacies, and why questions in AI.
